questions.text: In Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE), what determines which token pair gets merged next?
options: The pair with the highest mutual information score
The most frequently occurring adjacent pair in the corpus
The pair that maximizes the unigram language model likelihood
A randomly selected pair from the top-10 most frequent
questions.correct: The most frequently occurring adjacent pair in the corpus
questions.explanation: BPE iteratively merges the most frequently occurring adjacent pair of tokens. This is what distinguishes it from WordPiece, which uses likelihood-based merging.

questions.text: What is the key advantage of contextual embeddings (e.g., from BERT) over static embeddings (e.g., Word2Vec)?
options: Contextual embeddings produce different vectors for the same word depending on surrounding context
Contextual embeddings are always more accurate for word analogy tasks
Contextual embeddings don't require a fixed vocabulary
questions.correct: Contextual embeddings produce different vectors for the same word depending on surrounding context
questions.explanation: The defining feature of contextual embeddings is that the vector for a word changes based on its context. 'Bank' in 'river bank' gets a different embedding than 'bank' in 'bank account,' whereas Word2Vec assigns the same vector regardless of context.

questions.text: Why is the attention score divided by √d_k in the scaled dot-product attention formula?
options: To normalize the output vectors to unit length
To reduce computational cost by using smaller numbers
To prevent dot products from growing large and saturating the softmax function
To ensure the attention weights are always positive
questions.correct: To prevent dot products from growing large and saturating the softmax function
questions.explanation: When d_k is large, dot products can become very large in magnitude, pushing softmax outputs toward 0 and 1 (saturated regions where gradients vanish). Dividing by √d_k keeps the variance of scores at approximately 1, maintaining healthy gradient flow.

questions.text: In multi-head attention with d_model=512 and 8 heads, what is the dimension of each head's key vector (d_k)?
questions.explanation: In multi-head attention, the model dimension is split equally across heads: d_k = d_model / num_heads = 512 / 8 = 64. Each head operates on a 64-dimensional subspace, and the results are concatenated back to 512 dimensions.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the causal mask in decoder-only transformer models like GPT?
options: To reduce memory usage during training
To prevent each token from attending to future tokens in the sequence
To improve the quality of positional encodings
To filter out padding tokens in variable-length batches
questions.correct: To prevent each token from attending to future tokens in the sequence
questions.explanation: The causal mask sets attention scores to -∞ for all positions after the current token, ensuring that when predicting the next token, the model can only use information from previous tokens. This maintains the autoregressive property needed for text generation.
